overview: Jeskai Steel-Cutter is a tempo deck that leverages early mana acceleration to establish a wide board of creatures. It uses card advantage and land tutoring to outpace opponents while protecting its key finisher. The deck aims to overwhelm defenses through incremental damage and resilient land recursion.
win_conditions: Cori-Steel Cutter growing large through repeated combat damage triggers
Tamiyo, Inquisitive Student tutoring key lands and drawing cards to maintain board presence
Overwhelming the opponent with a wide board of low-cost creatures

keep_criteria: Hands with Mox Opal and a Mountain symbol land
Two-land hands with early interaction or cheap creatures
Tamiyo, Inquisitive Student paired with a land drop
Keep any 3+ land hand with Mox Opal, or any 4+ land hand with Opal + a turn-2 play (Tamiyo, Inquisitive Student, or a cheap artifact).
Keep 2-land hands only if they include Opal, a cantrip, and a turn-2 threat.
mulligan_criteria: Three-land hands with no early plays
Hands lacking Mountain symbols for Mox Opal
Heavy hands with only late-game cards
Mulligan hands with multiple Opals but no artifacts, or hands lacking Opal and a turn-2 play.

early_game: Play Mox Opal on turn one if possible to accelerate mana
Establish land drops and play cheap creatures to apply early pressure
Use early interaction to disrupt opponent's game plan
Turn 1: Play a land, cast Mox Opal if possible, or play a cheap artifact/land.
Turn 2: Activate Opal for one mana, cast Tamiyo or Inquisitive Student to dig for Cori-Steel Cutter or additional artifacts.
If drawing Opal + two artifacts, play both, activate Opal, and cast a turn-2 threat.
Sequence lands to ensure colored mana for Tamiyo/Student while maintaining artifact count for Opal.
mid_game: Cast Tamiyo, Inquisitive Student to draw cards and tutor for lands
Attack with multiple creatures to pump Cori-Steel Cutter with +1/+1 counters
Maintain board control while developing your advantage
Use Tamiyo/Student to cycle into answers or finishers rather than hoarding.
maintain a board presence of 1-2 artifacts plus your cutter.
Manage colored mana carefully—Jeskai can be mana-screwed without Opal.
late_game: Let Cori-Steel Cutter finish the game with a large attack
Recycle lands from the graveyard using Cori's death trigger or Tamiyo's tutor
Stabilize with card advantage and land recursion to outlast opponents
Cori-Steel Cutter is the primary win condition, often closing games through direct damage, exile effects, or artifact synergy.
Secondary finishers include Tamiyo’s ultimate or combo chains involving Inquisitive Student.
Go all-in when you have Opal online, a clear path to damage/exile, and the opponent has no known sweepers or counters.
Commit when your hand contains a finisher and you can protect it for one turn.

key_cards.name: Mox Opal
key_cards.notes: Play on turn one with a Mountain symbol land to enable early spells and creatures

key_cards.name: Tamiyo, Inquisitive Student
key_cards.notes: Use to draw cards, tutor for key lands, and provide mana dorking to support the board

key_cards.name: Cori-Steel Cutter
key_cards.notes: Attack with other creatures to trigger its growth ability, then attack with it for lethal damage. Use its death trigger to return lands from the graveyard.

sequencing_tips: Play Mox Opal before casting Tamiyo to maximize early mana availability
Coordinate attacks with multiple creatures to trigger Cori-Steel Cutter's growth ability efficiently
Use Tamiyo's tutor ability to find lands that enable Mox Opal or specific threats
Prioritize keeping Cori-Steel Cutter alive until it has accumulated enough counters to threaten lethal
Common mistake: playing Opal without enough artifacts to activate it, stalling your game plan.
Another: overcommitting to the board without considering sweepers.
Unique interaction: Cori-Steel Cutter likely scales with artifact count or has a triggered ability that synergizes with Tamiyo’s card draw.
Always track opponent’s graveyard and mana for combo tells.
Practice mulligan decisions rigorously—this deck is highly sensitive to opening hands.

matchups.advice: Play around early removal and board wipes by keeping interaction in hand
Disrupt their early game with interaction and avoid trading creatures into their recursion
Apply early pressure to prevent them from setting up their combo engine
Use Tamiyo to draw around their interaction and play around board wipes

Use cantrips and cheap artifacts to dig through counters. Cast Opal and threats in response to counterspells if possible. Rely on Tamiyo’s protection or flashback/graveyard recursion if available. Don’t overcommit to one threat; pressure from multiple angles forces counterspells to break.
Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use spot removal on key ramp pieces. Opal acceleration lets you race their ramp if you stabilize.
Prioritize cheap interaction and early board stalls. Tamiyo and Inquisitive Student can draw into answers. Don’t overextend into combat; trade efficiently and stabilize before going for the win.
Pressure their life total while digging for your win condition. Use Jeskai spells to trade resources favorably. Force them to use removal on your artifacts to buy time for Cori-Steel Cutter.

Win the resource war. Prioritize Opal and early artifacts to out-accelerate. Use Tamiyo to out-value their card draw. Sideboard in more interaction and finishers. Trade efficiently and punish overextensions.

common_threats: Watch for opponent’s mana development (ramp vs. control), graveyard activity (combo decks), and board state (sweepers vs. spot removal).
Key tells: opponent tapping for two mana on turn 2 often signals a combo or control plan
untapped blue mana late game usually means a counterspell or removal.
